And for text classification, usually what we would do is we would have a feature extractor from

For sequence labeling, normally what we do is we extract one vector for each thing that we would

we would predict something for each word. So this is an example of part of speech tagging,
but there's a lot of other sequence labeling tasks also. And what tasks exist for something
like sequence labeling? So sequence labeling is a pretty big subset of NLP tasks. You can express
a lot of things as sequence labeling. And basically given an input text x, we predict an output
label sequence y of equal length. So this can be used for things like part of speech tagging to
get the parts of speech of each word. It can also be used for something like limitization. And
limitization, basically what that is, is it is predicting the base form of each word.
And this can be used for normalization if you want to find like, for example, all instances of
a particular verb being used or all instances of a particular noun being used. This is a little
bit different than something like stemming. So stemming, normally what the stemming would do is it
would chop off the plural here. It would chop off s. But it wouldn't be able to do things like
normalize saw and to see because stemming just removes suffixes. It doesn't do any sort of normalization.
So that's the difference between limitization and stemming.
There's also something called morphological tagging. In morphological tagging, basically what
this is doing is this is a more advanced version of part of speech tagging that predicts things like,
okay, this is a past tense verb. This is a plural. This is a particular verb form and you have
multiple tags here. This is less interesting in English because English is kind of a boring
language morphologically. It doesn't have a lot of conjugation and other stuff, but it's a lot
more interesting and more complex languages like Japanese or Hindi or other things like that.
Chinese is even more boring than English, so if you're interested in Chinese, you don't need to worry

is span labeling. Here you want to predict spans and labels. This could be named entity recognition.

There's also things like syntactic chunking where you want to find all noun phrases and verb
phrases. Also semantic role labeling, where semantic role labeling is demonstrating who did what to
whom. So it's saying this is the actor, the person who did the thing, this is the thing that is being
done and this is the place where it's being done. So this can be useful if you want to do any sort
of analysis about who does what to whom other things like that. There's also a more complicated thing
